identity.title: Regulation and Administration of Communications, Electric, Gas, and Other Utilities

lens.included_activities: No qualifying lower-middle-market firms were identified inside this government-only NAICS classification; public utility commissions and government communications, electric, gas, water, and sewerage regulatory offices are retained only as operational analogues for workflow research.
lens.excluded_activities: Government regulatory bodies as captive public units, utility operators classified in Sector 22, private regulatory consultants and inspectors classified elsewhere, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Government offices administer, regulate, license, and inspect utilities through appropriations, assessments, or statutory fees rather than recurring outsourced-service contracts sold by a transferable firm to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS describes compliance officers across government and private employers rather than a wage-weighted occupation mix for utility regulators, while FERC reports federal regulatory workflows and volumes. I map rule and tariff research, filing intake, data validation, comparison, surveillance triage, report drafting, public-comment synthesis, scheduling, and routine license processing to AI exposure, while leaving inspections, hearings, stakeholder negotiation, enforcement judgment, technical engineering review, and accountable orders largely unexposed. The proxy may overstate exposure because utility matters involve specialized engineering and administrative-law review, or understate it where large agencies employ many filing, records, and analytics staff.
a.rationale: Regulatory administration combines structured filings, large datasets, repeat reviews, and document-heavy analysis with judgment-intensive proceedings and some field inspection. FERC already uses automated validations for quarterly submissions, so those realized efficiencies are excluded from the not-yet-automated opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and time study from federal and state utility commissions, matched to current e-filing, rules engines, surveillance analytics, document automation, and pilot AI systems by workflow.
caveats: No occupation mix specific to 926130 was found.
FERC is a federal energy regulator and does not represent state water, sewer, or communications agencies.
Existing automated data checks are not counted again as AI exposure.

rho.rationale: Electronic reporting, structured forms, automated validation, and market surveillance provide an installed base for additional AI, especially in intake, anomaly explanation, research, and drafting. Implementation will be limited by legacy systems, procurement, cybersecurity, confidential infrastructure information, administrative records, explainability, public-comment duties, engineering validation, and the need for commission or inspector accountability.
rho.change_evidence: Agency-level deployment data showing secure AI integration, active use, error and appeal rates, inspector and analyst time saved after review, auditability, procurement completion, and the percentage of filing types processed with validated assistance.
caveats: Digital filing and conventional analytics adoption do not directly measure generative-AI implementation.
Resources and legal authority vary widely among state and local commissions.
Implementation is applied only to the exposed opportunity in a.

e.bridge: Census defines 926130 exclusively as government establishments administering, regulating, licensing, and inspecting utilities and separately classifies utility operators. I translate that categorical scope into no eligible LMM firms because the frozen lens requires transferable firms with recurring external customers and excludes captive internal units. A rare quasi-public corporation might complicate ownership form, but genuine private consultants and contractors should be classified outside this public-administration industry.
e.rationale: The code captures sovereign regulatory agencies rather than commercial service providers. Appropriated or fee-funded commissions do not become eligible merely because they perform repeat work for regulated entities or the public.
e.change_evidence: Establishment-level NAICS and ownership records identifying a privately owned, revenue-bearing 926130 entity with recurring external customers and normalized EBITDA in the LMM band.
caveats: Private inspection, engineering, legal, and consulting firms may support regulators but belong to other NAICS codes.
The frozen dataset supplies no defensible LMM firm count.
Zero is classification-based rather than a neutral replacement for missing data.

s5.bridge: The Census source establishes government ownership and regulatory function, which I transform into no probability of a qualifying corporate-control sale. Appointments, elections, statutory reorganizations, jurisdiction transfers, or commission mergers do not convey ownership of a commercial operating firm. The conclusion could fail only if a privately controlled establishment were miscoded into the government-only classification.
s5.rationale: Regulatory agencies can change commissioners, mandates, budgets, or governmental level, but those events are not acquisitions of transferable firm control under the lens.
s5.change_evidence: Evidence of a privately controlled establishment legally classified in 926130 whose equity or operating control transferred in a qualifying transaction; governmental reorganizations would not qualify.
caveats: The value depends on correct NAICS classification.
Outsourcing selected regulatory support does not transfer control of the agency.
Changes in public leadership are excluded by definition.

q.rationale: No eligible commercial operator or customer contract exists in the government-only lens, so there is no defensible discounted share of implemented gross benefit retained after contractual pass-through, renewal pricing, or competition. Regulatory budgets and statutory fee assessments do not provide the commercial construct required by q.
q.change_evidence: Verified privately owned 926130 firms and their recurring service contracts, including pricing units, fee resets, savings-sharing terms, procurement competition, and realized renewal pricing after efficiency gains.
caveats: Budget retention and lower statutory assessments are not commercial operator retention.
Private adjacent providers are outside the industry code.
A zero would falsely imply observed full pass-through rather than an inapplicable commercial construct.

d5.bridge: EIA forecasts electricity demand and capacity, FERC reports federal energy-regulatory activity, and FCC documents one communications filing obligation; none directly measures constant-price demand for the full multi-utility regulatory service basket. I translate load growth, generation and pipeline buildout, market surveillance, recurring reporting, and communications licensing into modestly higher five-year regulatory quantity. The proxy may overstate demand if automation and streamlined rules reduce review per filing, or understate it if grid expansion, distributed resources, extreme weather, cyber risk, water infrastructure, and spectrum complexity add proceedings faster than utility volume grows.
d5.rationale: The regulated utility base is durable and becoming more complex. EIA expects sustained electricity demand and large capacity additions, while FERC's filing, audit, investigation, and surveillance volumes demonstrate recurring oversight. Efficiency can offset some growth, so the range begins near flat rather than simply tracking infrastructure expansion.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year workload forecasts from FERC, FCC, state public utility commissions, and water regulators covering rate cases, licenses, inspections, enforcement matters, interconnections, complaints, comments, pages and data volume, and staff hours per proceeding.
caveats: Energy demand and capacity are indirect drivers, not regulatory-service quantity.
Federal evidence underrepresents state and local water, sewer, and retail-rate regulation.
Policy changes can expand or contract workload independently of utility output.

o.bridge: Census establishes sovereign administration, licensing, and inspection functions, while FERC and FCC show automated validation, analytics, electronic filing, and accountable enforcement processes; none measures the future operator-required share. I infer that routine intake and monitoring can become more self-service, but nearly all remaining year-five quantity still requires an accountable public regulator to issue licenses and orders, conduct inspections, adjudicate disputes, and exercise enforcement authority. The proxy may overstate the current agency form if governments consolidate bodies or delegate more technical review, but not the need for public accountability.
o.rationale: Software can screen filings, flag anomalies, and generate analysis, but it cannot itself exercise statutory authority, balance stakeholder interests, conduct accountable field inspections, create an administrative record, or sign enforceable orders. A small lower tail allows elimination of routine clerical interactions through rules-based self-service.
o.change_evidence: Statutory and administrative-law changes permitting automated license issuance, rate decisions, inspection findings, or enforcement without accountable officials, plus audited deployments that survive rehearing and judicial review.
caveats: The accountable operator is a government agency, not an eligible commercial firm.
Private technical support can expand while final regulatory authority remains public.
The estimate applies only to quantity remaining after d5.

narrative.executive_view: Utility regulation has a credible AI workflow opportunity and durable demand, but 926130 is government-only and therefore contains no qualifying transferable LMM firms. Regulators process structured filings, analyze markets, draft orders, inspect facilities, and enforce complex rules; AI can improve the information layer, while sovereign authority and public accountability remain with the agency.
narrative.ai_impact: Likely applications include filing intake, data-quality checks, tariff and precedent research, public-comment synthesis, anomaly explanation, investigation triage, inspection planning, and draft reports or orders. FERC already uses automated validations and surveillance, so those benefits are not counted again. Engineering review, field inspection, contested hearings, enforcement judgment, and final decisions remain human-led.
narrative.value_capture: There is no commercial value-capture construct inside this NAICS code. An agency may convert savings into lower fees, reduced appropriations, faster case processing, or additional oversight, but none is retained gross benefit under a customer contract subject to renewal pricing and competition.
narrative.firm_availability: Census defines the industry as government establishments and explicitly places operating utilities elsewhere. Private consultants, inspectors, engineers, and software vendors may serve commissions, but they are outside 926130. Public appointments, reorganizations, or jurisdiction transfers are not qualifying control transactions.
narrative.demand_durability: Communications, electric, gas, water, and sewer systems remain essential regulated infrastructure. Electricity demand, generation capacity, data centers, distributed resources, pipelines, spectrum use, reliability, cybersecurity, and public participation can add complexity and workload even when automated filing and monitoring reduce effort per item.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive weakness is an empty commercial lens population. Operationally, AI adoption also faces legacy systems, confidential infrastructure and market data, administrative-record requirements, explainability, procurement, uneven state resources, and liability for missed violations or erroneous orders.
narrative.principal_driver: Structured filings, surveillance data, recurring reviews, and document-heavy proceedings create a substantial AI-addressable workflow surface.
narrative.principal_weakness: Government-only classification eliminates transferable firms and leaves commercial benefit retention undefined.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 926130 Regulation and Administration of Communications, Electric, Gas, and Other Utilities
sources.supports: Defines the industry as government establishments administering, regulating, licensing, and inspecting communications, electric, gas, water, and sewerage utilities and classifies utility operators elsewhere.

sources.title: Compliance Officers
sources.supports: Describes regulatory research and advice, policy development, risk assessment, audits, investigations, documentation, reporting, training, onsite inspection, and a workforce that is 37 percent government employed.

sources.title: FERC FY2025 Report on Enforcement
sources.supports: Documents investigations, audits, accounting proceedings, automated filing validation, market-data surveillance, enforcement actions, and quantified 2025 workload across federal energy regulation.

sources.title: FERC Natural Gas Industry Forms
sources.supports: Documents recurring annual and quarterly financial, operational, transaction, customer, facility, interruption, and system reporting, including electronic-only filings.

sources.title: EIA Forecasts Strongest Four-Year Growth in U.S. Electricity Demand Since 2000
sources.supports: Forecasts U.S. electricity use growth of 1 percent in 2026 and 3 percent in 2027, with data centers driving the strongest four-year demand growth since 2000.

sources.title: EIA Releases the Annual Energy Outlook 2026
sources.supports: Projects long-run electricity growth, generation-capacity additions of 50 to 90 percent by 2050, and substantial natural-gas production and pipeline infrastructure expansion across modeled cases.

sources.title: FCC Guard Band Licensee and Band Manager Annual Reports Due March 3, 2025
sources.supports: Shows recurring communications-license reporting, required information on service coverage and spectrum leases, electronic submission through the Universal Licensing System, and potential enforcement for nonfiling.
